title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
This report converts a draft defence value for money (VfM) framework into practical guidance for evaluators. It is grounded in Oxford Policy Management’s rubric-based approach to VfM assessment (King et al. 2023) and Verian’s Value for Investment guidance (King and Hurrell 2024), then adapts those ideas to defence capability, digital, and infrastructure programmes.
The central message is simple: defence VfM is not proved by a benefit-cost ratio alone. Evaluators need a transparent judgement about whether resource use is good enough in context, given the capability created, the risks carried, the alternatives available, and the value that is hard to monetise.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Evaluation Workflow
Verian’s Value for Investment process has two phases: design and implementation. The design phase covers understanding the programme, defining the value proposition and evaluation questions, setting criteria, setting standards, and determining the evidence needed. The implementation phase covers gathering evidence, analysing evidence, synthesising judgements through the criteria and standards, and reporting clear answers (King and Hurrell 2024).

1. Understand the Programme
Start with the programme, not with indicators. Establish the decision context, users, stakeholders, information needs, programme boundaries, lifecycle stage, and constraints. In defence, this should include the operational problem, acquisition route, industrial context, security and safety constraints, support model, dependencies with other programmes, and the decision the evaluation must inform.
Develop or review the theory of change at this stage. The theory of change should describe how defence resources and inputs are expected to support activities, outputs, outcomes, and wider impacts. For a defence programme, it should make the causal pathway explicit:

resources and inputs: funding, people, contracts, facilities, data, equipment, intellectual property, permissions, and relationships;
activities: design, procurement, build, integration, testing, training, deployment, support, adaptation, and assurance;
outputs: delivered equipment, software, services, facilities, trained users, doctrine, support arrangements, and data products;
outcomes: operational availability, decision advantage, deterrence, resilience, welfare, safety, interoperability, productivity, or estate quality;
impacts: national security effect, readiness, alliance contribution, sovereign choice, public value, industrial capacity, environmental performance, and future-force optionality.



2. Define the Value Proposition and Evaluation Questions
Extend the theory of change with a value proposition. Verian describes this as making value creation explicit: who the investment is valuable to, what resources are invested, how the investment converts resources into value, and what ways of working enable that value (King and Hurrell 2024).
For a defence programme, write a short value proposition covering:

who values the investment: front-line users, commands, maintainers, allies, suppliers, communities, taxpayers, and future programmes;
what value is expected: operational effect, resilience, deterrence, interoperability, safety, personnel welfare, industrial capacity, environmental value, sovereign options, and learning;
what resources are consumed: acquisition, support, workforce, facilities, data, training, assurance, opportunity cost, and through-life funding;
what alternatives exist: doing less, delaying, upgrading existing capability, buying off the shelf, partnering with allies, leasing, using commercial services, or choosing a non-materiel option;
what must be true for value to emerge: user adoption, integration, security, supportability, realistic training, supplier performance, governance, and credible threat or demand assumptions.

Turn this into a small number of evaluation questions. The main VfM question should ask whether the resource use is justified in context; subquestions should map to the criteria, standards, and evidence plan.
This follows OPM’s principle that VfM criteria must be specific to the programme rather than copied from generic definitions (King et al. 2023). It also reflects Verian’s argument that VfM assessment should integrate economic, social, environmental, cultural, and other public values rather than treating cost-benefit analysis as the whole answer (King and Hurrell 2024).


3. Set Criteria for Good Resource Use
Use the 5Es as a starting structure, then translate each criterion into defence language. Do not assume each E has equal weight in every programme.








Criterion
Defence interpretation
Evaluator prompt




Economy
Stewardship of inputs, contracts, workforce, facilities, support, data, and scarce specialist capacity.
Were the right inputs secured at appropriate quality and cost, with credible commercial discipline and risk control?


Efficiency
Conversion of resources into outputs through delivery, governance, integration, testing, learning, and relationships.
Did the programme avoid avoidable delay, rework, duplication, idle capacity, and transaction cost?


Effectiveness
Operational, organisational, and user value created by the delivered capability.
Does the capability work in the conditions that matter, with users able to exploit it?


Equity
Fair distribution of benefits, burdens, access, risks, and future liabilities.
Are personnel, commands, allies, suppliers, communities, taxpayers, and future budgets treated fairly?


Cost-effectiveness
Whether the total value created justifies the total resource use compared with credible alternatives.
Is the defence effect worth the acquisition, support, risk, opportunity, and through-life cost?



The 5Es are often most useful for ex-post or in-flight assessment. For ex-ante decisions, coordinate with the Green Book five-case model and use explicit criteria to make the strategic, economic, commercial, financial, and management cases easier to judge coherently.


4. Set Standards Before Interpreting Evidence
OPM’s practical contribution is the disciplined link from criteria to standards, evidence, and judgement (King et al. 2023). For each criterion, agree performance standards before judging the evidence.







Level
Defence VfM standard




Excellent
Performance exceeds reasonable expectations; trade-offs are explicit; evidence shows strong delivery of operational value, stewardship, learning, and fair distribution of burdens.


Good
Performance meets expectations with minor exceptions; material risks are controlled; evidence supports a credible positive judgement.


Adequate
Minimum expectations are met, but important weaknesses remain in cost, schedule, usability, integration, support, evidence, or distribution of burdens.


Poor
Minimum expectations are not met, or unresolved weaknesses make the use of resources hard to justify.



Rubrics should be specific enough to guide judgement. For example, “good effectiveness” for a satellite communications programme is not the same as “good effectiveness” for accommodation modernisation. The former may turn on assured communications, contested-environment resilience, and allied interoperability; the latter may turn on living conditions, occupancy, maintainability, and effects on personnel welfare.


5. Identify the Evidence and Methods Needed
Use economic analysis where it is feasible, but do not let it crowd out other evidence. Verian’s Value for Investment approach is useful for defence because many important effects are uncertain, classified, strategic, or difficult to monetise (King and Hurrell 2024).
Common evidence sources include:

business cases, approvals, commercial strategies, contract data, cost models, and cost assurance;
schedules, risk registers, issue logs, test results, acceptance evidence, and support data;
operational analysis, trials, exercises, user research, training records, and readiness data;
cyber, safety, security, resilience, interoperability, and data-governance evidence;
supplier, workforce, estate, environmental, community, and industrial-base evidence;
comparator evidence from allies, commercial alternatives, previous programmes, or portfolio options;
stakeholder evidence from users, front-line commands, maintainers, digital operators, commercial staff, suppliers, local communities, and allied partners.

At this point, decide whether cost-benefit analysis, cost-effectiveness analysis, break-even analysis, scenario analysis, or another economic method can make a useful contribution. Include CBA where the cost-benefit test is relevant, outcomes and costs can be credibly quantified and monetised, and the necessary data, skills, time, and ethical conditions are present. Where those conditions do not hold, use CBA only for the monetisable part of the value story or use a rubric-based VfM assessment as the main method.


6. Gather Evidence
Collect the agreed evidence in a way that follows the rubric. Survey, interview, document-review, trial-analysis, and administrative-data tools should use headings aligned to the criteria and subcriteria so evidence can be organised efficiently.
Defence evaluations should plan for practical constraints: classified evidence, operational security, small user populations, immature outcome data, long benefit horizons, supplier confidentiality, and contested causal claims. These constraints should be recorded as evidence limitations, not hidden in the final judgement.


7. Analyse Evidence
Analyse each evidence stream separately before synthesis. Cost data, schedule data, test evidence, operational analysis, user feedback, cyber assurance, estate data, and supplier evidence each need their own quality checks.
This is also where causal inference or contribution analysis should be handled. For some defence questions, a counterfactual may be available through options analysis, historical comparison, allied benchmarking, or status quo modelling. For others, a theory-based contribution claim will be more credible than forced monetisation.


8. Synthesize Judgements and Report
The final VfM judgement should show the reasoning chain:

programme context and theory of change;
value proposition and evaluation questions;
tailored criteria and subcriteria;
agreed standards;
evidence by criterion;
analysis, ratings, and uncertainty;
trade-offs between criteria;
overall judgement;
actions to improve future resource use.

Avoid averaging scores mechanically. Defence programmes often involve explicit trade-offs: higher acquisition cost may be justified by survivability, safety, interoperability, resilience, or lower through-life cost. The evaluator’s task is to explain whether those trade-offs are justified by the evidence.
Report the answer up front. The report should give clear evidence-supported ratings against the criteria, then show the reasoning and limitations behind those ratings.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Defence Criteria and Evidence Prompts

Economy: Resource Stewardship
Focus on whether the programme secured appropriate inputs at appropriate cost and quality.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Commercial discipline
Procurement strategy, competition records, sole-source justification, open-book evidence, price benchmarking, should-cost analysis.


Whole-life cost control
Acquisition, support, infrastructure, training, workforce, digital, disposal, decommissioning, and upgrade cost models.


Scarce capability inputs
Specialist engineering, nuclear, cyber, software, shipbuilding, aviation, construction, commercial, and safety skills.


Long-lead and obsolescence management
Long-lead item plans, spares, technology roadmaps, refresh cycles, design-change control, inventory strategy.


Security and quality investment
Assurance, test, certification, safety, cyber, data protection, and quality-at-source decisions that prevent later rework or failure.





Efficiency: Delivery and Learning
Focus on whether resources were converted into useful outputs without avoidable waste.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Schedule realism
Integrated master schedule, risk adjustment, critical path, dependency tracking, approvals history.


Integration and test flow
Systems integration evidence, trials plans, certification progress, defect and rework logs, acceptance evidence.


Governance speed
Decision logs, escalation routes, approvals time, change-control performance, delegated authority.


Supply-chain and resource flow
Supplier performance, material availability, yard or facility capacity, workforce allocation, competing programme demands.


Dynamic and relational efficiency
Learning logs, adaptation decisions, joint working arrangements, information sharing, dispute resolution, lessons applied across projects.





Effectiveness: Operational Value
Focus on whether the delivered outputs plausibly create intended defence outcomes.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Capability delivered
Acceptance trials, operational test and evaluation, performance against key parameters, mission-thread evidence.


Reliability, availability, maintainability
Availability data, defect rates, mean time between failures, support burden, spares availability, maintainer feedback.


Interoperability
Allied, joint, and legacy-system integration evidence; standards compliance; exercise results; data-sharing evidence.


User adoption and readiness
Training completion, user feedback, doctrine and tactics updates, simulator use, operational readiness indicators.


Resilience and adaptability
Cyber resilience, contested-environment performance, safety, upgrade path, modularity, technical debt, climate resilience where relevant.





Equity: Distribution of Benefits and Burdens
Focus on fairness across stakeholders and time.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Risk allocation
Contract terms, liability, incentives, safety responsibilities, cyber responsibilities, insurance, decommissioning obligations.


User and maintainer burden
Training load, supportability, operating cost, workload, safety, welfare, usability, deployment constraints.


Cross-force and allied access
Allocation across services, units, geographies, readiness priorities, allied interoperability, coalition use.


Industrial and workforce distribution
SME participation, regional effects, skills pipelines, sovereign capability, workshare, supplier resilience.


Inter-generational fairness
Technical debt, deferred maintenance, disposal liabilities, carbon, estate condition, future budget pressure.





Cost-Effectiveness: Value Relative to Alternatives
Focus on whether the outcomes justify the resources used.







Subcriterion
Defence evidence




Total cost of ownership
Acquisition, operation, sustainment, infrastructure, training, refresh, disposal, and opportunity cost.


Alternative options
Commercial service, allied collaboration, upgrade, lease, off-the-shelf buy, smaller scope, slower delivery, or non-materiel solution.


Strategic and operational value
Deterrence, resilience, readiness, interoperability, mission success, personnel welfare, information advantage.


Wider public value
Industrial capacity, exports, skills, environmental gains, community effects, cross-government reuse.


Uncertainty and sensitivity
Scenario analysis, optimism bias, cost risk, threat changes, demand changes, technology cycles.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
section: Applying the Guidance by Programme Type
text:
Applying the Guidance by Programme Type

Major Capability Platforms
For combat air, submarines, destroyers, armoured vehicles, and similar platforms, use the platform descriptions as adaptation prompts rather than generic case studies.







Programme type
VfM emphasis




Combat air
Integration of sensors, weapons, software, support, training, international partners, mission data, and through-life upgrade cost.


Submarines
Nuclear or specialist safety, stealth, industrial capacity, long-lead items, assurance, decommissioning liabilities, and strategic deterrence value.


Surface warships
Shipyard flow, combat-system integration, fleet availability, interoperability, crew burden, growth margins, and upgrade path.


Armoured vehicles
Protection, mobility, firepower, weight trade-offs, maintainability, soldier usability, fleet commonality, and alternative land-effect options.



For each platform, define what “good” means against the actual capability need, not against a generic list of desirable features.


Digital and Cyber Programmes
Digital programmes create value through data, connectivity, software, resilience, and user adoption. They need VfM criteria that recognise rapid technology cycles and cyber risk.
Skynet 6 illustrates the point. MOD describes it as the next-generation military satellite communications programme, with more than £5 billion being invested in the Skynet programme over ten years and objectives including resilient global services, space workforce development, prosperity, contested-space capability, and net zero (Ministry of Defence 2025). A VfM assessment should therefore examine commercial arrangements, satellite, ground and user segments, cyber and anti-jamming resilience, allied interoperability, workforce capacity, alternatives such as commercial SATCOM, and through-life replacement cycles.
LETacCIS is another useful example. MOD describes it as a land tactical communications programme delivered by Army Headquarters and Defence Digital’s Tactical Systems Service Executive with industry partners, including projects such as MORPHEUS, TRINITY, and Dismounted Situational Awareness (Ministry of Defence 2023). A VfM assessment should test whether modular delivery, multi-vendor coordination, open standards, data integrity, tactical usability, and cyber resilience are improving decision-making for users at reasonable cost.
Do not treat system uptime, agile velocity, or licence cost as sufficient VfM evidence by themselves. Digital VfM depends on whether users can exploit the system securely and reliably in operational conditions.


Infrastructure and Estate Programmes
Infrastructure programmes create value through places where personnel live, train, work, and operate. They need VfM criteria that account for long asset lives, planning and environmental constraints, communities, maintainability, and user experience.
The Defence Estate Optimisation portfolio is the main public example. MOD describes it as Defence’s largest estates change programme, investing £5.1 billion in modern, greener, and sustainable infrastructure, delivering accommodation for over 27,000 personnel and families, releasing surplus land for more than 32,000 homes, and forecasting over 200 hectares of biodiversity net-gain measures (Ministry of Defence 2026). Evaluation should therefore combine cost and schedule evidence with estate rationalisation, personnel outcomes, biodiversity, land-disposal value, community effects, and future estate liabilities. The NAO’s report on optimising the defence estate provides an important accountability source for judging delivery risks and assumptions (National Audit Office 2021b).
Single Living Accommodation Modernisation shows the need to evaluate outputs and outcomes separately. DIO reported that Project SLAM built or refurbished more than 22,800 bedspaces across 52 locations, with a construction spend of nearly £1.3 billion and extensive use of modular construction (DIO Communications Team 2015). The NAO later found persistent single living accommodation problems, including low-quality accommodation and under-occupancy (National Audit Office 2021a). A VfM judgement should therefore ask not only whether rooms were delivered efficiently, but whether the accommodation system as a whole produced sustained improvements in quality, occupancy, welfare, retention, and maintenance burden.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Reporting Template
Use a consistent reporting structure so readers can audit the judgement:

Decision context: What decision will the VfM assessment support?
Theory of change: How are resources expected to create defence outputs, outcomes, and wider impacts?
Value proposition: What value was expected, for whom, through what mechanisms, and compared with what alternatives?
Evaluation questions: What answers do decision-makers and stakeholders need?
Tailored criteria: How were the 5Es translated into programme-specific definitions?
Standards: What would poor, adequate, good, and excellent performance look like?
Evidence and methods: What evidence was used, what was missing, and how credible is it?
Analysis and judgements: What rating is given for each criterion, and why?
Trade-offs and uncertainty: Which weaknesses are tolerable, which are material, and which undermine VfM?
Overall conclusion: Is resource use justified in context?
Improvement actions: What should change in delivery, future approvals, commercial strategy, data, evaluation, or portfolio decisions?

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Common Risks

Retrofitting standards after seeing the evidence.
Treating cost control as the whole of VfM.
Ignoring users, maintainers, cyber operators, safety specialists, or local communities.
Counting industrial or strategic benefits without evidence that they are additional.
Reporting a single overall rating without explaining trade-offs and uncertainty.
Using generic 5E definitions that do not reflect the actual defence capability.
Confusing delivery outputs with sustained operational or welfare outcomes.

title: Defence VfM Guidance
text:
Conclusion
The strongest defence VfM evaluations combine OPM’s explicit criteria, standards, evidence, and judgement discipline with Verian’s broader Value for Investment framing. That combination is well suited to defence because the value of a programme often lies in resilience, deterrence, interoperability, safety, welfare, sovereign choice, and future optionality as well as monetised benefits.
Evaluators should therefore design VfM assessment as a transparent reasoning process. The question is not simply whether the programme spent less, delivered more, or produced a favourable ratio. It is whether the total resource use was justified by the operational, organisational, strategic, social, environmental, and future value created.

This note compares definitions of value for money from UK government appraisal guidance, the National Audit Office, and the OECD DAC evaluation criteria (HM Treasury 2026, 2022; National Audit Office 2026, 2013; OECD 2019, 2021).

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Core Idea
Authoritative definitions agree that value for money is a judgement about resources and results, not a single financial metric. The sources differ in emphasis:

HM Treasury frames VfM as a balanced appraisal judgement about public resources, objectives, social value, monetisable and unmonetisable effects, distribution, and risk.
The NAO frames VfM as the optimal use of resources through economy, efficiency, and effectiveness to achieve intended outcomes.
The OECD DAC criteria frame evaluation as a judgement of merit or worth, with efficiency asking whether resources are justified by results.

Together, these definitions support a broad but disciplined view of VfM. A credible VfM note should explain what objectives matter, what resources were used, what results were achieved, what effects were not monetised, and how trade-offs were judged.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Comparison of Definitions









Source
Short definition
Main emphasis
Practical implication




Green Book
“balanced judgement about the optimal use of public resources”
Social value, objectives, monetised and unmonetised effects, distribution, risk
Do not rank options by benefit-cost ratio alone. Show the full judgement.


Green Book supplementary guidance
“finding the best way to use public resources”
Applying the Green Book method and five case model
Tie VfM to objectives, strategic fit, economic appraisal, and decision advice.


NAO
“optimal use of resources” to achieve intended outcomes
Economy, efficiency, effectiveness
Test whether spending was used well in delivering policy objectives.


OECD DAC
Criteria are “not a methodology or approach”
Evaluation questions and lenses for judging interventions
Use criteria thoughtfully; do not treat them as a mechanical checklist.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
What the Definitions Share
All three institutional framings require explicit objectives. An option or intervention cannot be good value if it does not achieve the purpose it was meant to serve.
They also require more than cost minimisation. The NAO’s economy criterion asks whether inputs were obtained at the right cost and quality, but its definition also includes efficiency and effectiveness. HM Treasury is even more explicit that monetisable costs and benefits are only part of the judgement. OECD similarly treats efficiency as one lens among relevance, coherence, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability.
The common pattern is:

Define the objectives and decision context.
Identify the resources used or proposed.
Assess results, benefits, costs, quality, risk, and distribution.
Explain the judgement, including trade-offs and uncertainty.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Differences in Emphasis
The Green Book is strongest for appraisal. It is designed to help decision makers choose between options before implementation, using social cost-benefit analysis or social cost-effectiveness analysis where appropriate.
The NAO definition is strongest for public audit. It looks at whether government has used money well in delivering policy objectives, while not questioning the policy objectives themselves.
The OECD DAC criteria are strongest for evaluation design and synthesis. They broaden the judgement beyond resource conversion alone, especially where relevance, coherence, impact, sustainability, or equity concerns affect whether an intervention is worth continuing or scaling.

title: VfM Definitions
text:
Practical Implications
For value for money evaluation, these definitions imply that the evaluator should:

State the VfM definition being used.
Link the definition to the decision or accountability question.
Avoid treating a benefit-cost ratio as the whole VfM answer.
Include non-monetised benefits, costs, risks, and distributional effects where they matter.
Use criteria or rubrics to make the final judgement traceable.

The most useful definition is therefore not just a sentence. It is a working framework for deciding what evidence must be collected and how mixed evidence will be interpreted.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
This note summarises the OECD DAC evaluation criteria using the OECD’s official evaluation criteria page and the OECD DAC Network on Development Evaluation publications that revised and explained the criteria (OECD 2019, 2021).
The related source pages are:

OECD: Evaluation Criteria
OECD: Better Criteria for Better Evaluation

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Core Idea
The OECD DAC criteria are a shared framework for judging the merit, worth, or significance of an intervention. They are not a method in themselves. They define the main evaluative questions that an evaluation may need to answer.
The criteria work as complementary lenses. Together, they help evaluators examine whether an intervention is appropriate to the context, fits with other activity, achieves results, uses resources well, produces important wider effects, and leaves benefits that can continue.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
The Six Criteria
The revised OECD DAC criteria are relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability.

Relevance asks whether the intervention is doing the right things. It examines how far objectives and design respond to needs, policies, priorities, and changing circumstances.
Coherence asks how well the intervention fits. It examines compatibility with other interventions, policies, standards, actors, and systems in the same context.
Effectiveness asks whether the intervention is achieving its objectives. It examines achieved or expected results, including differential results across groups.
Efficiency asks how well resources are being used. It examines whether results are delivered economically and in a timely way.
Impact asks what difference the intervention makes. It examines significant positive or negative, intended or unintended, higher-level effects.
Sustainability asks whether the benefits will last. It examines whether the net benefits of the intervention continue or are likely to continue.

The 2019 revision added coherence as a distinct criterion and updated the other definitions to support more nuanced analysis, including attention to equity, environmental effects, synergies, and trade-offs.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Principles for Use
The OECD gives two principles for applying the criteria. First, they should be applied thoughtfully, with their meaning interpreted in light of the intervention, stakeholders, context, and evaluation questions. Second, their use should depend on the purpose of the evaluation. An evaluation does not need to cover every criterion mechanically.
This means the criteria should be selected and adapted before analysis begins. The evaluation design should explain which criteria matter most, how each criterion is interpreted for the intervention, and what evidence would support a credible judgement.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Practical Implications for VfM Evaluation
For value for money evaluation, the DAC criteria help broaden the judgement beyond cost and impact alone. Efficiency is directly relevant to resource use, but it is only one lens. A low-cost intervention may still represent poor value if it is not relevant, duplicates other work, produces weak or inequitable results, creates harmful wider effects, or leaves benefits that cannot be sustained.
The criteria are especially useful when they are turned into explicit evaluation questions, indicators, evidence standards, or rubrics. This makes the judgement traceable: readers can see how evidence under each criterion contributes to the overall conclusion.
The OECD framing is relevant to definitions of VfM because it treats evaluation criteria as lenses for judging interventions. The criteria are “not a methodology or approach”; they define questions that evaluations need to answer. For VfM, this means efficiency should usually be interpreted alongside relevance, coherence, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability rather than isolated as a narrow resource-use metric.

title: OECD DAC Evaluation Criteria
text:
Limits and Risks
The criteria do not replace evaluation design. They do not decide the method, identify the counterfactual, define evidence quality, or explain how trade-offs should be weighed. Those choices still need to be made explicitly.
A common risk is treating the criteria as a checklist. A stronger evaluation uses them to focus inquiry: selecting the criteria that matter for the decision, defining clear questions, assessing evidence quality, and explaining how mixed findings have been synthesised.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Core Idea
The Magenta Book frames value for money evaluation around a practical question: whether an intervention is a good use of public resources. That question cannot be answered by impact evidence alone. Impact evaluation can estimate what changed, but VfM evaluation asks whether the outcomes, costs, unintended effects, and alternatives justify the use of resources.
The guidance treats VfM as a balanced judgement about public value. Monetised costs and benefits matter, but the judgement should also account for important benefits and costs that cannot be monetised or placed in a benefit-cost ratio.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Relationship to Appraisal
Magenta Book VfM evaluation is closely connected to Green Book appraisal. A standard VfM evaluation compares the costs and benefits achieved by the intervention with the expectations set out in the business case or impact assessment.
A more developed evaluation can go further by comparing different ways of achieving the same strategic objective. This makes VfM evaluation useful for both accountability and learning: it can show whether appraisal assumptions held up in practice and whether future business cases should adjust their forecasts.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Planning Principles
The Magenta Book gives four practical design principles for VfM evaluation:

Plan VfM from the outset, alongside process and impact evaluation.
Define what good value means before the analysis begins.
Link the evaluation back to appraisal forecasts and business case assumptions.
Build from observed data wherever possible, while being clear when forecasts are needed for longer-term benefits or costs.

The design implication is that cost, process, outcome, and contextual data need to be collected deliberately. A late VfM exercise may be forced to rely on incomplete cost data, weak attribution, or assumptions that cannot be tested.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Common Methods
The Magenta Book identifies social cost-benefit analysis as a widely used method when the main costs and benefits can be quantified and monetised. This can produce summary measures such as benefit-cost ratios, and it can compare options with different types of outcomes.
Other related approaches include social cost-effectiveness analysis, cost utility analysis, and social return on investment. These are most useful when the evaluation can measure enough of the intervention’s costs and outcomes to support credible comparison.
The guidance is clear that monetised findings need context. A reported ratio or net present value should sit alongside evidence on major unmonetised benefits, unmonetised costs, limitations, uncertainty, and omitted effects.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Four Es Framework
Where monetisation is incomplete or unsuitable, the Magenta Book describes the Four Es framework as an additional way to structure VfM assessment:

Economy: whether inputs were purchased or obtained at appropriate cost and quality.
Efficiency: whether resources were converted into outputs or outcomes well.
Effectiveness: whether the intervention achieved its intended outcomes.
Equity: whether benefits, costs, and access were distributed fairly.

The Four Es can organise mixed quantitative and qualitative evidence, but they do not usually produce a comparable numerical summary. They are criteria for making a judgement, not a substitute for explaining how evidence has been weighed.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Rubric-Based Approaches
Annex A explicitly recognises rubric-based VfM assessment. A rubric defines criteria and performance standards, often in a table, so evaluators can judge whether observed performance is poor, adequate, good, excellent, or another agreed standard.
The method is especially useful when outcomes are intangible, hard to monetise, or spread across several dimensions of public value. The rubric should be co-designed early, aligned with the Theory of Change, and agreed before evidence is interpreted. This reduces the risk of inventing standards after results are known.
Rubrics do not generate VfM metrics by themselves. Their value is that they provide a transparent way to interpret mixed evidence and synthesize it into an overall evaluative judgement.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Link to OPM King
The Magenta Book’s treatment of rubric-based approaches is closely aligned with the OPM King VfM Guidance. Both approaches treat VfM as explicit evaluative judgement rather than only financial calculation.
The Annex A discussion also cites Julian King’s OPM guide and gives examples of government evaluations using Four Es and rubric-based frameworks. This makes the OPM King approach part of the recognised UK government evaluation methods landscape, especially for interventions where monetisation is difficult or partial.

title: Magenta Book VfM Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications
For evaluators, the Magenta Book points to a staged workflow:

Start from the decision context and appraisal assumptions.
Decide which benefits and costs can be credibly monetised.
Identify important non-monetised effects that still matter for public value.
Select a method or combined framework that fits the intervention and evidence base.
Define criteria and standards early, especially if using Four Es or rubrics.
Gather empirical cost, process, output, outcome, and distributional evidence.
Report both summary measures and judgement-based findings with limitations.

The most credible VfM evaluations make the judgement traceable. Readers should be able to see how appraisal expectations, observed evidence, monetised analysis, unmonetised effects, uncertainty, and stakeholder-relevant criteria combine into the final conclusion.

title: DfT VfM Indicator

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
This note summarises DfT’s value for money indicator collection (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on what the indicator reports and what it does not report.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money indicator

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
Core Idea
The DfT value for money indicator reports the proportion of approved project spending that is assessed as high or very high value for money under DfT’s framework. It is a transparency measure about investment decisions, not an evaluation of realised project performance.
This distinction is central. The indicator is based on expected value at the point of approval. It does not show whether schemes later delivered the benefits, costs, distributional effects, or non-monetised impacts assumed in the appraisal.

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
How the Indicator Works
The indicator aggregates approved spending by value for money category. Projects are weighted by approved spending, so large lower-value projects can materially affect the indicator even if many smaller projects are high value.
The category used in the indicator comes from DfT’s appraisal process. The process starts from monetised evidence such as BCRs and then considers impacts, risks, and non-monetised factors before assigning a final category.
The indicator therefore depends on the quality of the underlying appraisal evidence, the consistency of category assignment, and the treatment of uncertainty.

title: DfT VfM Indicator
text:
What It Does Not Show
The indicator should not be read as evidence of achieved value for money. It does not compare forecast benefits with realised benefits after implementation. It also does not show whether optimism bias, demand forecasts, cost risks, or non-monetised impacts were borne out in practice.
This makes it useful for understanding DfT’s appraisal and decision pipeline, but limited for evaluation. A separate evaluation would be needed to assess whether the approved portfolio actually delivered high or very high value once implemented.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
This note summarises two DfT value for money assessment examples: Integrated Transport Block funding and major bus-related schemes (Department for Transport 2016a, 2016b).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Integrated transport block funding: value for money assessment
GOV.UK: Value for money assessment for major bus-related schemes

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Core Idea
These examples show DfT applying value for money logic to portfolios or groups of transport schemes rather than to a single business case. They are useful because they expose the practical compromises in aggregating appraisal evidence across many local or modal interventions.
The examples are still primarily appraisal-based. They estimate approximate value from available scheme evidence, costs, benefits, assumptions, and sensitivity tests. They are not full ex post evaluations of realised value.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Integrated Transport Block
The Integrated Transport Block assessment examined local authority capital funding for smaller transport improvement schemes. The source is useful because small schemes often have weaker appraisal, monitoring, and evaluation evidence than major projects.
The assessment therefore has to work with uneven local evidence. It uses survey evidence, available appraisal material, monitoring or evaluation evidence where present, and sensitivity tests to estimate approximate VfM.
For evaluators, the important lesson is that evidence systems matter. If local bodies do not collect consistent data on costs, outputs, outcomes, and benefits, later VfM assessment becomes dependent on assumptions and partial evidence.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Major Bus-Related Schemes
The major bus-related schemes assessment reviewed schemes approved under funds including Local Majors, Local Sustainable Transport Fund, and Better Bus Areas. It reported an aggregate BCR and translated that into a DfT value for money judgement.
The report is explicit that only monetised benefits and costs are included in the aggregate analysis, while non-monetised benefits and costs are handled qualitatively in the wider appraisal process. This is a useful example of the boundary between monetised portfolio metrics and broader VfM judgement.
For evaluation, the key question is whether the expected benefits from bus priority, interchange, reliability, passenger growth, decongestion, and wider impacts were realised after implementation.

title: DfT VfM Case Examples
DfT VfM Case Examples
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
Both examples can be used as starting points for retrospective evaluation. The evaluator would need to reconstruct the original appraisal assumptions, gather delivery and outcome evidence, and compare expected and realised value.
Where monetised data are weak, a structured criteria or rubric approach may be needed. The OPM King VfM Guidance is useful here because it gives a way to combine monetised evidence, qualitative evidence, standards, and judgement transparently.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:

The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Transport analysis guidance

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s Transport Analysis Guidance (TAG) landing page and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: Transport analysis guidance
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Core Idea
TAG is DfT’s transport-specific analytical system for modelling and appraisal. It gives practitioners guidance on defining problems, developing options, building transport models, estimating impacts, and presenting appraisal evidence for business cases.
For value for money work, TAG is the technical base. It supplies the methods and data assumptions that feed the DfT VfM Framework and the economic dimension of the DfT Transport Business Case.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Evaluation Practitioner Guidance
TAG includes guidance for the evaluation practitioner. This matters because DfT treats evaluation as part of the transport appraisal process rather than a disconnected afterthought.
The implication is that evaluation should be planned early enough to collect baseline data, monitor delivery, and compare realised effects with the assumptions used in appraisal. Evaluation can then improve future appraisal by testing demand forecasts, cost assumptions, behaviour change assumptions, benefit realisation, and the treatment of non-monetised impacts.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Analytical Assurance
TASM maintains TAG, develops value for money guidance, scrutinises value for money advice submitted to ministers and DfT investment boards, and oversees analytical assurance. This gives the guidance an institutional role: TAG is both a technical method base and a quality-assurance standard for decision advice.
For evaluators, the assurance role is important because weaknesses in modelling, data, or assumptions can become evaluation questions. A good evaluation can ask whether the appraisal method was proportionate, transparent, and empirically supported.

title: DfT TAG Appraisal and Evaluation
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
TAG is strongest as appraisal guidance. It supports ex ante decisions by estimating expected costs and benefits before delivery. Evaluation uses a different evidence posture: it asks what happened, why, for whom, at what cost, and how this compares with expectations.
The link between the two is learning. TAG assumptions should not be treated as settled truths. They are hypotheses that can be tested through monitoring, process evaluation, impact evaluation, and value for money evaluation.
This connects TAG to the Magenta Book VfM Evaluation, which gives central government guidance on using evaluation evidence to judge actual value after implementation.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s transport business case guidance (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on how the business case process uses appraisal evidence, Transport Analysis Guidance, the Green Book, and DfT’s value for money framework.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: Transport business case guidance

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s transport business case guidance (Department for Transport 2022). It focuses on how the business case process uses appraisal evidence, Transport Analysis Guidance, the Green Book, and DfT’s value for money framework.
The related source page is:

GOV.UK: Transport business case guidance

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Core Idea
DfT uses the five-case model to structure transport investment decisions. Value for money sits mainly in the economic dimension, but the guidance is clear that the five dimensions are connected. A proposal can only be strong if it has strategic fit, economic value, commercial viability, financial affordability, and credible delivery arrangements.
The business case is therefore not just a container for a BCR. It is an iterative decision document that should become more accurate as evidence develops from strategic outline case to outline business case to full business case.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Business Case Dimensions
The five dimensions create a practical frame for VfM:

Strategic dimension: whether the proposal responds to a real transport problem and fits national, regional, local, and organisational priorities.
Economic dimension: whether the proposal is the best choice for maximising social welfare, using options appraisal and DfT’s value for money framework.
Commercial dimension: whether the market, procurement route, and supply-side capacity can deliver the proposal.
Financial dimension: whether the proposal is affordable within the relevant budgets.
Management dimension: whether the proposal can be delivered and managed from outputs through to outcomes.

This matters for VfM evaluation because weak delivery, affordability, or commercial assumptions can undermine expected value even when the appraisal ratio looks strong.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
section: Role of TAG and the VfM Framework
text:
Role of TAG and the VfM Framework
The guidance positions TAG as the detailed transport appraisal and modelling guidance used to produce consistent evidence for business cases. DfT’s value for money framework then explains how appraisal outputs are converted into a VfM assessment and communicated to decision makers.
The economic dimension should include economic, environmental, and social impacts. It should use monetised evidence where possible, but also account for qualitative and quantitative evidence where monetisation is not credible or complete. This aligns with the Green Book VfM Guidance and the DfT VfM Framework.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Strategic Coherence and Distribution
The 2022 guidance reflects the Green Book Review emphasis on strategic coherence, distributional analysis, and place-based impacts. This is important for transport because schemes often have uneven effects across people, places, transport users, non-users, businesses, and future generations.
For evaluation, this means that the business case should not be treated only as a forecast of aggregate benefits. It is also a record of whose outcomes mattered, where benefits and costs were expected to fall, and which strategic objectives the intervention was supposed to advance.

title: DfT Transport Business Case
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
The business case sets the expected value story. Evaluation tests that story.
The management dimension is especially important because it should describe how outputs become outcomes and how benefits will be realised. A later VfM evaluation can use the business case to ask:

Were the original objectives and critical success factors still relevant?
Were the short-listed options and counterfactual assumptions reasonable?
Did costs, benefits, risks, and delivery assumptions hold up?
Were non-monetised and distributional effects tracked after approval?
Did commercial, financial, or management issues affect achieved value?

The Magenta Book VfM Evaluation provides the stronger evaluation framing for this comparison between expected and realised value.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s value for money framework and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division in maintaining value for money guidance (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money framework

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
This note summarises the Department for Transport’s value for money framework and the role of the Transport Appraisal and Strategic Modelling division in maintaining value for money guidance (Department for Transport 2025, 2026).
The related source pages are:

GOV.UK: DfT value for money framework
DfT: Transport appraisal and strategic modelling division

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
Core Idea
DfT’s framework is an appraisal framework for judging whether expected costs are justified by expected benefits. It is designed for decision advice on proposed transport interventions, especially where ministers and investment boards need a clear value for money statement.
The framework sits inside the wider government appraisal system. It draws on the Green Book VfM Guidance, uses DfT’s Transport Analysis Guidance for transport-specific appraisal methods, and feeds the economic dimension of the DfT Transport Business Case.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
VfM Metrics
The framework starts from monetised appraisal. Benefit-cost ratios (BCRs), net present values, present value of benefits, and present value of costs are central because they give decision makers a disciplined way to compare expected social benefits with expected resource costs.
DfT also recognises that a single monetised ratio is incomplete. Transport proposals can have important environmental, social, place-based, distributional, safety, reliability, resilience, and landscape effects that may not be fully monetised. The VfM judgement therefore needs to combine monetised evidence with qualitative and quantitative evidence on non-monetised impacts.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
VfM Categories
DfT communicates results through value for money categories. The category is not simply the initial BCR band. Analysts are expected to consider non-monetised impacts, risk, uncertainty, sensitivity tests, and the robustness of the analysis before recommending a final category.
This makes the category a structured judgement. The BCR provides the starting point, but the final judgement should explain whether other effects strengthen, weaken, or materially change the initial conclusion.

title: DfT VfM Framework
DfT VfM Framework
text:
Relationship to Evaluation
The DfT framework is mainly ex ante. It guides decisions before schemes are approved and implemented. It does not itself provide a full method for ex post value for money evaluation.
However, it creates a clear evaluation agenda. If appraisal expects benefits from journey time savings, reliability, safety, mode shift, carbon reduction, local growth, or distributional effects, later evaluation should test whether those benefits were delivered and whether the appraisal assumptions were credible.
This links directly to the Magenta Book VfM Evaluation note. The Magenta Book is more explicit about evaluating actual costs, outcomes, unintended effects, and alternatives after implementation. DfT’s framework supplies the original appraisal expectations that evaluation can test.

title: DfT VfM Framework
text:
Practical Implications
For transport evaluators, the framework is useful as a baseline for reconstructing the original value proposition:

Identify the original VfM category and its monetised basis.
Separate monetised impacts from non-monetised impacts.
Trace the assumptions that drove the BCR or net present value.
Check whether sensitivity tests covered the risks that later mattered.
Compare expected and realised costs, benefits, and distributional effects.
Record lessons for future TAG parameters, optimism bias, and appraisal practice.

Where effects cannot be monetised convincingly, evaluators may need a rubric-based or criteria-based synthesis. That is where the OPM King VfM Guidance complements DfT’s category framework.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
This note summarises HM Treasury’s Green Book definition of value for money, using the 2026 Green Book and the 2022 Green Book supplementary guidance on value for money (HM Treasury 2026, 2022).
The related source pages are:

HM Treasury: The Green Book (2026)
HM Treasury: Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
This note summarises HM Treasury’s Green Book definition of value for money, using the 2026 Green Book and the 2022 Green Book supplementary guidance on value for money (HM Treasury 2026, 2022).
The related source pages are:

HM Treasury: The Green Book (2026)
HM Treasury: Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Value for Money

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Core Idea
The Green Book defines value for money as a balanced judgement about “the optimal use of public resources” to achieve a proposal’s objectives.
This makes VfM broader than choosing the option with the highest benefit-cost ratio. The preferred option must meet the proposal’s objectives and optimise social value within the constraints faced by decision makers.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Social Value
The Green Book links value for money to social value. Social value includes the benefits, costs, and risks that affect the wellbeing of the UK population, not only direct financial returns to government.
This is why appraisal needs to consider effects on people, places, public bodies, businesses, households, charities, and the environment where those effects are material. Public value and social value are closely related terms in this guidance.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Factors in the Judgement
The Green Book definition identifies several factors that should inform the VfM judgement:

Performance against the proposal’s objectives and critical success factors.
Monetisable social costs and benefits.
Unmonetisable social costs and benefits.
Public sector financial impact.
Distribution of costs and benefits across social groups and places.
Risk and uncertainty.

The implication is that a summary metric can inform but not replace judgement. Benefit-cost ratios, net present social value, and other measures must be interpreted alongside unmonetised effects and uncertainty.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Supplementary Guidance
The Green Book supplementary guidance says VfM is about “finding the best way to use public resources” to deliver policy objectives. It explains the role of the five case model and shows how strategic and economic dimensions interact.
The guidance is especially useful for explaining why the highest benefit-cost ratio is not automatically the best VfM option. Decision advice can justify selecting a different option when objectives, unmonetisable factors, risk, affordability, or strategic fit make that option stronger overall.

title: Green Book VfM Guidance
text:
Practical Implications
For evaluators and analysts, the Green Book framing means that a VfM assessment should:

Start with clear objectives and critical success factors.
Compare realistic options, including business as usual where relevant.
Present monetised and unmonetised effects together.
Explain risk, uncertainty, and distributional effects.
Show why the preferred option is the balanced judgement, not only the highest ratio.

This definition is particularly useful when linking ex post evaluation back to appraisal. Evaluation can test whether the social value, costs, risks, and distributional assumptions used in a business case held up in practice.

text:
Links

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
This note summarises the National Audit Office definition of value for money, using the NAO’s public description of its work and its VFM diagram (National Audit Office 2026, 2013).
The related source pages are:

National Audit Office: About us
National Audit Office: VFM diagram

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
This note summarises the National Audit Office definition of value for money, using the NAO’s public description of its work and its VFM diagram (National Audit Office 2026, 2013).
The related source pages are:

National Audit Office: About us
National Audit Office: VFM diagram

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Core Idea
The NAO defines good value for money as “the optimal use of resources” to achieve intended outcomes.
This definition is grounded in public audit. The NAO reports to Parliament on whether government is delivering value for money and examines how public money has been spent. Its role is to assess delivery and resource use, not to question the policy objectives set by government.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
The Three Es
The NAO definition is structured around economy, efficiency, and effectiveness:

Economy concerns the cost and quality of resources used as inputs.
Efficiency concerns the relationship between outputs and the resources used to produce them.
Effectiveness concerns whether intended and unintended outcomes are achieved.

The VFM diagram links resources, inputs, outputs, and outcomes. It shows that cost-effectiveness and value for money depend on the relationship between resources and outcomes, not only on whether spending was low.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Public Audit Framing
The NAO framing is practical and accountability-focused. It asks whether public bodies have used resources well in delivering policy objectives, whether services could be improved, and whether recommendations can help government achieve better value.
This makes the definition well suited to reviews of implementation, programme management, service delivery, procurement, and operational performance. It is less focused on selecting between options before implementation than the Green Book, but it is highly relevant to judging actual delivery.

title: NAO VfM Definition
text:
Practical Implications
For value for money evaluation, the NAO definition implies that the evaluator should:

Identify the intended outcomes and the resources used.
Distinguish economy, efficiency, and effectiveness evidence.
Avoid treating savings as VfM unless quality and outcomes are protected.
Explain whether weaknesses are due to objectives, delivery, resources, governance, or external factors.
Make findings useful for accountability and improvement.

A low-cost intervention can still be poor value if it produces weak outcomes. A costly intervention can still represent good value if it delivers important outcomes efficiently and effectively relative to the resources used.

text:

text:
This note summarises the second edition of OPM’s value for money guidance, Assessing Value for Money: The Oxford Policy Management Approach (King et al. 2023). The guide was written by Julian King, Daniel Wate, Esther Namukasa, Alex Hurrell, Frances Hansford, Patrick Ward, and Shiva Faramarzifar.

title: OPM King VfM Guidance
text:
One-Page Summary
OPM defines value for money as good resource use. VfM asks how well resources are used and whether that use is justified. The guide is explicit that VfM cannot be measured by a single formula, even though numeric and economic evidence can be important.
The practical contribution of the approach is a disciplined process for making evaluative judgements. It uses criteria to define the aspects of performance that matter, standards to define different levels of performance, and mixed evidence to support a transparent judgement. This makes VfM a reasoning process: evidence is organised, interpreted against agreed standards, and synthesised into a performance story.
The guidance uses the FCDO/NAO 5Es as a useful starting point: economy, efficiency, effectiveness, equity, and cost-effectiveness. These are not fixed labels to copy into every framework. Each criterion should be translated into programme-specific definitions and subcriteria. The same word, such as efficiency, can mean different things in a training programme, an adaptive governance programme, a market systems programme, or a humanitarian response.
The approach is intended to be integrated with monitoring, evaluation, and learning systems. VfM frameworks should use existing evidence where it is relevant, avoid duplicating MEL processes, and support adaptive management. The strongest VfM reports show the chain of reasoning from criteria to standards, evidence, judgement, and recommendations for improving resource use.
